content:
# Leviticus Chapter 27 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 75r–75v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 113–114
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_027.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 27 is the formal appendix to the entire book — a supplementary legislative section on voluntary vows and their monetized redemption, following the climactic blessings-and-curses colophon of chapter 26. Where chapter 26 sealed the Holiness Code with its threefold Sinai-formula ("statutes and judgments and torot"), chapter 27 extends the same legislative authority to a practically urgent topic: what happens when Israelites voluntarily dedicate persons, animals, houses, and land to YHWH, and later need to reclaim them?

The chapter's architectural logic moves through a spectrum of holiness from fully redeemable (persons, v.8; houses, v.15; unclean animals, v.13; agricultural tithe, v.31) through conditionally redeemable (ancestral land, vv.19–20; purchased land, v.23) to absolutely irrevocable (firstborn animals, v.26; anything placed under cherem, vv.28–29; pastoral tithe once counted, v.33). The cherem — total, irrevocable dedication — sits at the extreme end of this spectrum as the most holy category in the entire cultic system.

The chapter's closing verse (v.34) is the final colophon of the entire book of Leviticus, sealing all 27 chapters under the authority of the Sinai covenant.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | erekh = "valuation" | ki yafli neder = "makes a special vow" | sheqel ha-qodesh = "sheqel of the sanctuary" | chamishito = "a fifth" (the 20% surcharge) | cherem = "devoted thing" (preserve; do not render anathema) | qodesh-qodashim = "most holy" | ma'aser = "tithe" | bekhor = "firstborn" | yovel = "Jubilee" | achuzah = "possession/holding" | sedeh miqnatoh = "a field he has bought" | mot yumat = "shall surely be put to death" | Moses (English name form)

---

## Consensus Translation

**27:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**27:2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: When a man makes a special vow, according to your valuation of persons to the LORD,

**27:3** your valuation shall be: for a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old, your valuation shall be fifty shekels of silver by the shekel of the sanctuary.

**27:4** And if it is a female, your valuation shall be thirty shekels.

**27:5** And if the person is from five years old up to twenty years old, your valuation for a male shall be twenty shekels and for a female ten shekels.

**27:6** And if the person is from a month old up to five years old, your valuation for a male shall be five shekels of silver, and for a female your valuation shall be three shekels of silver.

**27:7** And if the person is sixty years old and upward, for a male your valuation shall be fifteen shekels and for a female ten shekels.

**27:8** But if he is too poor for your valuation, then he shall be presented before the priest, and the priest shall value him; according to what the one who vowed can afford, the priest shall value him.

**27:9** "And if it is from the animals from which one may bring an offering to the LORD, all that he gives from it to the LORD shall be holy.

**27:10** He shall not exchange it or substitute for it, a good for a bad or a bad for a good; and if he does indeed substitute one animal for another, then it and its substitute shall both be holy.

**27:11** And if it is any unclean animal from which one may not bring an offering to the LORD, then he shall present the animal before the priest,

**27:12** and the priest shall value it, whether it is good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall be.

**27:13** But if he redeems it, he shall add a fifth to your valuation.

**27:14** "And when a man dedicates his house as holy to the LORD, the priest shall value it, whether it is good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall stand.

**27:15** And if the one who dedicated it redeems his house, he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it, and it shall be his.

**27:16** "And if a man dedicates to the LORD some of the field of his possession, your valuation shall be according to its seed: a homer of barley seed at fifty shekels of silver.

**27:17** If he dedicates his field from the year of the Jubilee, your valuation shall stand.

**27:18** But if he dedicates his field after the Jubilee, then the priest shall calculate for him the silver according to the years that remain until the year of the Jubilee, and it shall be deducted from your valuation.

**27:19** And if the one who dedicated the field redeems it, he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it, and it shall revert to him.

**27:20** But if he does not redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another man, it shall not be redeemed anymore.

**27:21** And the field, when it goes out in the Jubilee, shall be holy to the LORD as a devoted field; it shall become the priest's possession.

**27:22** "And if a man dedicates to the LORD a field that he has bought, which is not a part of the field of his possession,

**27:23** then the priest shall calculate for him the amount of the valuation up to the year of the Jubilee, and he shall give the valuation on that day — it is holy to the LORD.

**27:24** In the year of the Jubilee the field shall return to the one from whom he bought it, to him whose possession is the land.

**27:25** And every valuation shall be by the shekel of the sanctuary: twenty gerahs shall be the shekel.

**27:26** "But the firstborn of the animals, which as a firstborn belongs to the LORD — no man may dedicate it; whether ox or sheep, it is the LORD's.

**27:27** And if it is among the unclean animals, then he shall buy it back at your valuation and add a fifth to it; and if it is not redeemed, it shall be sold at your valuation.

**27:28** "But any devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD from all that is his — from human and animal and from the field of his possession — shall not be sold and shall not be redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.

**27:29** No devoted person who is devoted for destruction shall be redeemed; he shall surely be put to death.

**27:30** "And every tithe of the land — from the seed of the land, from the fruit of the tree — it belongs to the LORD; it is holy to the LORD.

**27:31** And if a man redeems some of his tithe, he shall add a fifth to it.

**27:32** And of every tithe of the herd and the flock — every tenth animal of all that passes under the rod — the tenth shall be holy to the LORD.

**27:33** He shall not inspect whether it is good or bad, and he shall not substitute for it; and if he does indeed substitute for it, then it and its substitute shall both be holy — it shall not be redeemed."

**27:34** These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS and PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention throughout.

**V. 2 — "Special vow" vs. "extraordinary vow."** EMET: "makes a special vow." LOGOS: "makes a special vow." PNEUMA: "makes an extraordinary vow." Ruling: "makes a special vow" — EMET/LOGOS. Both capture the Hifil of p-l-' (yafli). PNEUMA's "extraordinary" is not wrong but "special vow" is the more established formal rendering and both EMET and LOGOS agree.

**V. 2 — "Persons to the LORD" vs. "dedicating a person to the LORD."** EMET: "according to your valuation of persons to the LORD." LOGOS: "the persons shall be yours by valuation to the LORD." PNEUMA: "dedicating a person to the LORD, the valuation shall be set according to the following scale." Ruling: EMET's phrasing is the most formally accurate to be-erkekha nefashot la-YHWH. LOGOS's "shall be yours by valuation" introduces an interpretation not required by the MT. PNEUMA restructures the sentence significantly. Consensus adopts EMET's construction: "according to your valuation of persons to the LORD."

**V. 2 — PNEUMA restructures vv. 2–7 as a single prose block.** PNEUMA combines v. 2's introduction with vv. 3–7's valuation table into one run-on passage. EMET and LOGOS maintain verse-by-verse structure. Consensus follows EMET/LOGOS with each verse separately rendered.

**V. 3 — "Your valuation shall be" (opening).** EMET: "then your valuation shall be: for a male from twenty years old and up to sixty years old, your valuation shall be fifty sheqels of silver." LOGOS: "If your valuation is of a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old, your valuation shall be fifty shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "For a male from twenty to sixty years old, the valuation is fifty silver shekels by the sanctuary standard." Ruling: "for a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old, your valuation shall be fifty shekels of silver by the shekel of the sanctuary" — drawing on EMET's structure and LOGOS's phrasing; both preserve the repeated erkekha. "Shekels" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) vs. "sheqels" (EMET) — consensus uses "shekels" as the standard English form.

**V. 3 — "Sheqels/shekels" throughout.** EMET uses "sheqels" (Hebrew spelling); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "shekels" (standard English). Consensus: "shekels" — standard English form, consistent throughout.

**V. 3 — "By the shekel of the sanctuary" vs. "by the sanctuary standard."** EMET: "by the sheqel of the sanctuary." LOGOS: "according to the shekel of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "by the sanctuary standard." Ruling: "by the shekel of the sanctuary" — EMET/LOGOS. "The sanctuary standard" (PNEUMA) is interpretive; the Hebrew specifies "the shekel" (ha-sheqel) as the unit.

**V. 4 — "And if she is a female" vs. "And if it is a female."** EMET: "And if she is a female." LOGOS: "If it is a female." PNEUMA: "For a female in that same range, thirty shekels." Ruling: "And if it is a female" — LOGOS. The MT has ve-im neqevah hi (if female she); "it is" is cleaner than "she is" in this legal-taxonomic context since the subject is the valuation category, not a specific person. Vav-conjunction preserved.

**V. 5 — "If the person is" vs. "And if from five years old."** EMET: "And if from five years old and up to twenty years old, your valuation for a male shall be twenty shekels and for a female ten shekels." LOGOS: "If the person is from five years old up to twenty years old, your valuation shall be for a male twenty shekels, and for a female ten shekels." PNEUMA: "For a male from five to twenty years old, twenty shekels; for a female, ten shekels." Ruling: blend of EMET/LOGOS — "And if the person is from five years old up to twenty years old, your valuation for a male shall be twenty shekels and for a female ten shekels." PNEUMA's telegraphic table-style drops the legal formula structure.

**V. 6 — "For a female your valuation shall be three shekels" vs. "for a female infant."** EMET and LOGOS: "for a female your valuation shall be three shekels of silver." PNEUMA: "for a female infant, three shekels." Ruling: "for a female your valuation shall be three shekels of silver" — EMET/LOGOS. "Infant" (PNEUMA) is an interpretive addition; the MT does not specify "infant" even for the one-month-to-five-years bracket.

**V. 7 — "And upward" vs. "sixty years old or over" vs. "above."** EMET: "from sixty years old and upward." LOGOS: "sixty years old or over." PNEUMA: "from sixty years old and above." Ruling: "sixty years old and upward" — EMET. The MT has va-ma'lah ("and upward"), a specific directional idiom; "and upward" is most precise.

**V. 7 — "And if the person is" vs. "And if from sixty."** EMET begins "And if from sixty years old and upward." LOGOS: "And if the person is sixty years old or over." PNEUMA: "For a male from sixty years old and above." Ruling: "And if the person is sixty years old and upward" — combining LOGOS's "the person is" subject with EMET's "and upward" directional.

**V. 8 — "Too poor for your valuation."** EMET: "too poor [makh] for your valuation." LOGOS: "too poor to pay your valuation." PNEUMA: "cannot afford the set valuation." Ruling: "too poor for your valuation" — EMET. The MT has makh hu me-erkekha ("he is poor/low from your valuation"); "too poor for" is the precise idiom. LOGOS's "to pay" and PNEUMA's "set" are both added glosses.

**V. 8 — "Presented before the priest" vs. "made to stand before."** EMET: "he shall stand before the priest [ve-he'emido lifnei ha-kohen]." LOGOS: "he shall be made to stand before the priest." PNEUMA: "he is to bring the one being dedicated before the priest." Ruling: "he shall be presented before the priest" — a middle path between EMET's "stand" and LOGOS's "made to stand." The Hifil he'emido means "cause to stand / present." PNEUMA introduces "the one being dedicated" as a separate subject, which restructures the sentence.

**V. 8 — "According to what the one who vowed can afford."** EMET: "according to what the one who vowed can afford." LOGOS: "according to what the one who vowed is able to afford." PNEUMA: "according to what the one who vowed can afford." Ruling: "according to what the one who vowed can afford" — EMET/PNEUMA. LOGOS's "is able to afford" is slightly verbose.

**V. 8 — "The priest shall value him" (repeated at verse end).** EMET and LOGOS both repeat "the priest shall value him" at the end of v. 8. PNEUMA adds "The priest adjusts the price to what the person can pay" — a second sentence paraphrase. Consensus: EMET/LOGOS repetition maintained; no paraphrase.

**V. 9 — "From the animals from which one may bring an offering."** EMET: "from the animals from which one brings an offering to the LORD." LOGOS: "an animal that may be offered as an offering to the LORD." PNEUMA: "an animal of the kind that may be offered to the LORD." Ruling: "from the animals from which one may bring an offering to the LORD" — adjusted EMET with modal "may" for the imperfect yaqrivu. LOGOS's "as an offering" preserves the qorban term; PNEUMA drops it.

**V. 9 — "All that he gives from it to the LORD shall be holy."** EMET: "all that he gives from it to the LORD shall be holy." LOGOS: "all such that any man gives to the LORD is holy." PNEUMA: "any such animal given to the LORD becomes holy." Ruling: "all that he gives from it to the LORD shall be holy" — EMET. The MT's kol asher yitten mimmenah la-YHWH yihyeh-qodesh specifies mimmenah ("from it/from that category"), which LOGOS drops. PNEUMA's "becomes holy" is dynamic but correct; "shall be holy" is more formally precise.

**V. 10 — "Exchange it or substitute for it."** EMET: "He shall not exchange it [lo yachalifennu] or swap it [ve-lo yamir oto]." LOGOS: "He shall not exchange it or substitute for it." PNEUMA: "The person must not swap it or exchange it." Ruling: "He shall not exchange it or substitute for it" — LOGOS. Two verbs (chalif and mir) are preserved. PNEUMA reverses the order and uses "swap" for the first verb; EMET uses "swap" for yamir, which is the less established English equivalent.

**V. 10 — "A good for a bad or a bad for a good."** All three agree on this phrase. Consensus: "a good for a bad or a bad for a good" — unanimous.

**V. 10 — "If he does indeed substitute" — intensifying infinitive absolute.** EMET: "and if he does indeed swap [ve-im hamor yamir]." LOGOS: "and if he does in any way substitute one animal for another." PNEUMA: "If they do exchange one animal for another." Ruling: "and if he does indeed substitute one animal for another" — EMET's infinitive absolute force preserved ("does indeed") combined with LOGOS's "one animal for another" for clarity.

**V. 10 — "It and its substitute shall both be holy."** EMET: "then it and its substitute shall both be holy." LOGOS: "both it and the substitute shall be holy." PNEUMA: "both the original and its substitute become holy." Ruling: "then it and its substitute shall both be holy" — EMET. "Its substitute" (LOGOS/EMET) preserves the possessive of temuratoh; PNEUMA's "the original" is interpretive.

**V. 11 — "From which one may not bring an offering."** EMET: "of which one does not bring as an offering to the LORD." LOGOS: "that may not be offered as an offering to the LORD." PNEUMA: "one that cannot be offered to the LORD." Ruling: "from which one may not bring an offering to the LORD" — the negative parallel to v. 9. LOGOS's "as an offering" preserves the qorban; "may not" captures the prohibition force of lo yaqrivu.

**V. 11 — "He shall present the animal before the priest."** EMET: "he shall present the animal before the priest." LOGOS: "he shall present the animal before the priest." PNEUMA: "the person is to bring the animal before the priest." Ruling: "he shall present the animal before the priest" — EMET/LOGOS agree.

**V. 12 — "Whether it is good or bad" vs. "as either good or bad."** EMET: "whether it is good or bad." LOGOS: "as either good or bad." PNEUMA: "high or low." Ruling: "whether it is good or bad" — EMET. The MT has bein tov u-vein ra' ("between good and between bad") — a judicial quality-assessment formula, not a pricing range. PNEUMA's "high or low" applies the idiom used in v. 14 (where it recurs) but here the MT uses tov/ra' not high/low.

**V. 12 — "As the priest values it, so it shall be."** EMET: "according to your valuation, O priest [ke-erkekha ha-kohen], so it shall be." LOGOS: "as the priest values it, so it shall be." PNEUMA: "whatever the priest says, that is what it stands at." Ruling: "as the priest values it, so it shall be" — LOGOS. EMET's "O priest" is an interesting vocative reading of ha-kohen but is not the most natural rendering here; LOGOS's "as the priest values it" conveys the same meaning more cleanly. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the valuation terminology.

**V. 13 — "But if he redeems it."** EMET: "But if he redeems it [ve-im ga'ol yig'alennu]." LOGOS: "But if he wishes to redeem it." PNEUMA: "If the owner wants to buy it back." Ruling: "But if he redeems it" — EMET. The MT's infinitive absolute ga'ol intensifying yig'alennu does not express mere desire ("wishes to" — LOGOS) but the action itself. "Buy it back" (PNEUMA) substitutes padah-language for ga'al-language unnecessarily.

**V. 13 — "He shall add a fifth to your valuation."** EMET: "then he shall add a fifth to your valuation." LOGOS: "he shall add a fifth to your valuation." PNEUMA: "he must add a fifth of the assessed value on top." Ruling: "he shall add a fifth to your valuation" — EMET/LOGOS. "On top" (PNEUMA) is an explanatory addition. "A fifth" for chamishito is correct throughout; see also vv. 15, 19, 27, 31.

**V. 14 — "Dedicates his house as holy to the LORD."** EMET: "consecrates his house as holy to the LORD." LOGOS: "dedicates his house as a holy thing to the LORD." PNEUMA: "dedicates their house as holy to the LORD." Ruling: "dedicates his house as holy to the LORD" — EMET/PNEUMA's phrasing over LOGOS's "a holy thing" (which adds the indefinite article unnecessarily; the Hebrew qodesh is a predicate adjective/noun appositively).

**V. 14 — "Whether it is good or bad."** EMET: "whether it is good or bad." LOGOS: "as either good or bad." PNEUMA: "high or low." Ruling: "whether it is good or bad" — EMET. Same as v. 12: the MT uses tov/ra' here, not high/low.

**V. 14 — "As the priest values it, so it shall stand."** EMET: "as the priest values it, so it shall stand." LOGOS: "as the priest values it, so it shall stand." PNEUMA: "whatever the priest assesses it at, that is what it stands at." Ruling: "as the priest values it, so it shall stand" — EMET/LOGOS agree. "Stand" (yaqum) is the correct verb (distinct from v. 12's "be" / yihyeh) — EMET/LOGOS both correctly distinguish.

**V. 15 — "The one who dedicated it" vs. "the man who dedicates it."** EMET: "the one who consecrated it." LOGOS: "the man who dedicates it." PNEUMA: "the one who dedicated it." Ruling: "the one who dedicated it" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT's ha-maqddish is a participle ("the one consecrating/who consecrated"); not specifically "the man" as LOGOS specifies.

**V. 15 — "He shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation."** EMET: "he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it." LOGOS: "he shall add a fifth to the valuation price." PNEUMA: "he must add a fifth to the assessed price." Ruling: "he shall add a fifth of the silver of your valuation to it" — EMET. The MT's chamishit kesef-erkekha specifies "the silver of your valuation" (kesef-erkekha), which LOGOS/PNEUMA collapse to "valuation price." "Silver of your valuation" is the more precise formal rendering.

**V. 15 — "And it shall be his."** EMET: "and it shall belong to him." LOGOS: "and it shall be his." PNEUMA: "and it will be his again." Ruling: "and it shall be his" — LOGOS. "Again" (PNEUMA) is an addition beyond the MT.

**V. 16 — "Some of the field of his possession."** EMET: "some of the field of his holding [sedeh achuzato]." LOGOS: "part of the land that is his possession." PNEUMA: "a portion of his inherited land." Ruling: "some of the field of his possession" — EMET/adjusted. Achuzah is "possession/holding" — the inalienable ancestral land. LOGOS's "land" for sedeh (specifically "field") is a loss; PNEUMA's "inherited" is interpretive (though accurate). "Field of his possession" preserves both sedeh and achuzah.

**V. 16 — "According to its seed: a homer of barley seed at fifty shekels."** EMET: "according to its seed [le-fi zar'o]: a homer of barley seed [zera' omer se'orah] at fifty shekels of silver." LOGOS: "in proportion to its seed: a homer of barley seed at fifty shekels of silver." PNEUMA: "the value shall be set according to how much seed it takes to sow it — fifty shekels of silver for every homer of barley seed." Ruling: "according to its seed: a homer of barley seed at fifty shekels of silver" — EMET. "Homer" is the standard unit. LOGOS's "in proportion to its seed" is defensible; PNEUMA's explanatory addition "how much seed it takes to sow it" goes beyond the MT.

**V. 17 — "From the year of the Jubilee" vs. "from the year of Jubilee."** EMET: "from the year of the jubilee [mi-shenat ha-yovel]." LOGOS: "from the year of Jubilee." PNEUMA: "during the Year of Jubilee." Ruling: "from the year of the Jubilee" — EMET. The definite article ha-yovel is present in the MT. Jubilee is capitalized as a proper title.

**V. 17 — "Your valuation shall stand" vs. "it shall stand at your full valuation."** EMET: "your valuation shall stand [be-erkekha yaqum]." LOGOS: "it shall stand at your full valuation." PNEUMA: "the full assessed value stands." Ruling: "your valuation shall stand" — EMET. The MT has be-erkekha yaqum — "by your valuation it shall stand." "Full valuation" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) adds "full," interpreting the force of the statement; the MT text itself is sufficient.

**V. 18 — "Then the priest shall calculate for him the silver."** EMET: "then the priest shall calculate for him the silver [ve-chishav-lo ha-kohen et-ha-kesef]." LOGOS: "then the priest shall calculate the price according to the years that remain." PNEUMA: "the priest will calculate the price proportionally, according to how many years remain." Ruling: "then the priest shall calculate for him the silver according to the years that remain until the year of the Jubilee" — EMET's "the silver" preserved (kesef is explicit in MT); LOGOS's structural framing is adopted.

**V. 18 — "It shall be deducted from your valuation."** EMET: "and it shall be deducted from your valuation [ve-nigar me-erkekha]." LOGOS: "and the valuation shall be reduced." PNEUMA: "and reduce the assessed value accordingly." Ruling: "and it shall be deducted from your valuation" — EMET. The MT's nigar (Niphal of g-r-', "to be diminished/deducted") is a specific verb meaning deduction; "reduced" (LOGOS) is acceptable but less precise. "From your valuation" (me-erkekha) is explicit in the MT.

**V. 19 — "And if the one who dedicated the field redeems it."** EMET: "And if the one who consecrated the field redeems it." LOGOS: "And if he who dedicates the field wishes to redeem it." PNEUMA: "If the one who dedicated it wants to buy it back." Ruling: "And if the one who dedicated the field redeems it" — EMET/adjusted. The MT's infinitive absolute ga'ol intensifying yig'al expresses the action, not the wish; LOGOS's "wishes to" and PNEUMA's "wants to" both add desiderative meaning not present.

**V. 19 — "It shall revert to him."** EMET: "it shall revert to him [ve-qam lo]." LOGOS: "it shall remain his." PNEUMA: "the field reverts to him." Ruling: "it shall revert to him" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT's ve-qam lo has a sense of standing/reverting; "remain his" (LOGOS) could suggest it never left, which misses the reversion force after consecration. "Revert" is more precise.

**V. 20 — "It shall not be redeemed anymore."** EMET: "it shall no longer be redeemed [lo yig'al od]." LOGOS: "it shall not be redeemed anymore." PNEUMA: "it can no longer be redeemed." Ruling: "it shall not be redeemed anymore" — LOGOS. All three agree in substance; "anymore" (LOGOS) best renders od.

**V. 21 — "When it goes out in the Jubilee."** EMET: "when it goes out in the jubilee [be-tsetoh va-yovel]." LOGOS: "when it is released in the Jubilee." PNEUMA: "when that field is released in the Jubilee." Ruling: "when it goes out in the Jubilee" — EMET. The MT's be-tsetoh ("in its going out") is a specific idiom of release/departure at the Jubilee; "released" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is interpretive. "Goes out" preserves the yatsa' vocabulary.

**V. 21 — "As a devoted field" vs. "like a field that has been devoted."** EMET: "as a devoted field [ki-sedeh ha-cherem]." LOGOS: "like a field that has been devoted." PNEUMA: "like a field that has been devoted and put under the ban." Ruling: "as a devoted field" — EMET. The MT's ki-sedeh ha-cherem is compact: "like a devoted-field / like a field of cherem." LOGOS's "that has been devoted" unpacks the construct chain adequately but adds words. PNEUMA's "put under the ban" is explanatory addition, anticipating vv. 28–29.

**V. 21 — "It shall become the priest's possession."** EMET: "it becomes the priest's holding [achuzato]." LOGOS: "the possession thereof shall be the priest's." PNEUMA: "it passes into the priest's possession." Ruling: "it shall become the priest's possession" — blending LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT's la-kohen tihyeh achuzato ("to the priest it shall be his possession") uses achuzah — the inalienable land-term — now applied to the priest. "Possession" renders achuzah consistently with v. 16 and throughout.

**V. 22 — "A field that he has bought, which is not a part of the field of his possession."** EMET: "a field that he has bought [sedeh miqnatoh], which is not from the field of his holding [lo mi-sedeh achuzato]." LOGOS: "a field that he has bought, which is not a part of the field of his possession." PNEUMA: "a field he purchased rather than inherited." Ruling: "a field that he has bought, which is not a part of the field of his possession" — LOGOS. The critical distinction between miqnah (purchase) and achuzah (ancestral holding) must be explicitly stated; PNEUMA collapses this into "rather than inherited" which loses the miqnah terminology.

**V. 23 — "The amount of the valuation."** EMET: "the amount of your valuation [et michsat ha-erakh]." LOGOS: "the amount of the valuation." PNEUMA: "the value proportionally." Ruling: "the amount of the valuation" — LOGOS. Michsat ha-erakh is the "reckoned/portioned amount of the valuation"; LOGOS is accurate. PNEUMA's "proportionally" is an adverb added beyond the MT.

**V. 23 — "He shall give the valuation on that day."** EMET: "and he shall give your valuation on that day [ba-yom ha-hu]." LOGOS: "the man shall give the valuation on that day." PNEUMA: "the person pays that amount on the spot." Ruling: "and he shall give the valuation on that day" — EMET. "On that day" (ba-yom ha-hu) is the specific temporal marker, not "on the spot" (PNEUMA).

**V. 23 — "It is holy to the LORD."** EMET: "it is holy to the LORD." LOGOS: "as a holy thing to the LORD." PNEUMA: "as something holy to the LORD." Ruling: "it is holy to the LORD" — EMET. The MT's qodesh la-YHWH is a predicate statement ("holy to the LORD"), not "as a holy thing."

**V. 24 — "The field shall return to the one from whom he bought it."** EMET: "the field shall return to the one from whom he bought it [la'asher qanahu me-itto]." LOGOS: "the field shall return to him from whom it was bought." PNEUMA: "the field reverts to the one it was bought from." Ruling: "the field shall return to the one from whom he bought it" — EMET. "From whom he bought it" preserves the active construction (the dedicant is the subject of buying); LOGOS's passive "from whom it was bought" loses that agent.

**V. 24 — "To him whose possession is the land."** EMET: "to him whose ancestral holding is the land [la'asher-lo achuzat ha-aretz]." LOGOS: "to whom the land belongs as a possession." PNEUMA: "to the original owner of the land." Ruling: "to him whose possession is the land" — EMET/adjusted. The MT's la'asher-lo achuzat ha-aretz specifically uses achuzah for the original owner's inalienable relationship to the land. LOGOS's rendering is adequate; PNEUMA's "original owner" adds "original" which is not explicit in the Hebrew. "To him whose possession is the land" preserves the achuzah terminology.

**V. 25 — "Every valuation shall be by the shekel of the sanctuary."** EMET: "And all your valuations shall be by the sheqel of the sanctuary." LOGOS: "Every valuation shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary." PNEUMA: "Every valuation is to be set in sanctuary shekels." Ruling: "And every valuation shall be by the shekel of the sanctuary" — EMET/LOGOS combined. "Sanctuary shekels" (PNEUMA) is a compressed form that loses the genitive "of the sanctuary" (ha-qodesh).

**V. 25 — "Twenty gerahs shall be the shekel."** All three agree. PNEUMA: "twenty gerahs to the shekel." Ruling: "twenty gerahs shall be the shekel" — EMET/LOGOS. The MT's esrim gerah yihyeh ha-sheqel has the verb yihyeh ("shall be"); PNEUMA drops the verb.

**V. 26 — "Which as a firstborn belongs to the LORD."** EMET: "which is made firstborn to the LORD [asher yevukkar la-YHWH]." LOGOS: "which as a firstborn belongs to the LORD." PNEUMA: "because the firstborn already belongs to the LORD." Ruling: "which as a firstborn belongs to the LORD" — LOGOS. The Pual yevukkar expresses the firstborn's already-established divine belonging; LOGOS's "as a firstborn belongs to" is precise. EMET's "made firstborn" is technically accurate for the Pual passive but unusual in English.

**V. 26 — "No man may dedicate it" vs. "no man shall consecrate it."** EMET: "no man shall consecrate it." LOGOS: "no man may dedicate it." PNEUMA: "No one may dedicate a firstborn animal to the LORD." Ruling: "no man may dedicate it" — LOGOS. The modal "may" correctly captures the prohibition. EMET's "shall" is less natural for a prohibition ("you shall not" vs. "you may not"). PNEUMA's "no one" softens the ish ("man") of the MT.

**V. 26 — "Whether ox or sheep."** EMET: "whether cattle or sheep [im shor im seh]." LOGOS: "whether ox or sheep." PNEUMA: "Whether ox or sheep." Ruling: "whether ox or sheep" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. Shor = ox/head of cattle (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "cattle" (EMET) is a collective but shor is singular.

**V. 27 — "He shall buy it back at your valuation."** EMET: "he shall buy it back [u-fedahu] by your valuation." LOGOS: "he shall buy it back at your valuation." PNEUMA: "the owner may buy it back by adding a fifth to its assessed value." Ruling: "he shall buy it back at your valuation and add a fifth to it" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA embeds the "adding a fifth" into the first clause and then omits the second clause's structure.

**V. 28 — "Any devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD."** EMET: "any devoted thing [kol-cherem] that a man devotes [yacharem] to the LORD." LOGOS: "no devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD." PNEUMA: "nothing that a person devotes to the LORD." Ruling: "any devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD" — EMET. The MT begins with kol-cherem asher yacharem ish ("every devoted thing that a man devotes"); the prohibitive force comes from "shall not be sold / shall not be redeemed." LOGOS restructures to "no devoted thing...shall be" making "no" do the negating work. Both approaches are defensible; EMET's "any...shall not" mirrors the Hebrew more directly.

**V. 28 — "From human and animal."** EMET: "from human and animal [me-adam u-vehemah]." LOGOS: "whether man or animal." PNEUMA: "whether a human being, an animal." Ruling: "from human and animal" — EMET. The MT's me-adam u-vehemah is a partitive compound; "from human and animal" preserves the distributive structure. "Whether...or" (LOGOS) is an alternative phrasing.

**V. 28 — "And from the field of his possession."** EMET: "and from the field of his holding [u-mi-sedeh achuzato]." LOGOS: "or of his possession in the land." PNEUMA: "or inherited land." Ruling: "and from the field of his possession" — EMET. The MT's u-mi-sedeh achuzato specifies "field" (sedeh) — LOGOS drops sedeh. "Inherited land" (PNEUMA) is interpretive.

**V. 28 — "Shall not be sold and shall not be redeemed."** EMET: "shall not be sold [lo yimmaker] and shall not be redeemed [ve-lo yig'al]." LOGOS: "shall be sold or redeemed." PNEUMA: "may be sold or bought back." Ruling: "shall not be sold and shall not be redeemed" — EMET. The MT has two separate negated verbs; "shall not...and shall not" preserves this double prohibition. LOGOS collapses them into one clause with "or."

**V. 28 — "Every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD."** EMET: "every devoted thing is most holy [qodesh-qodashim] to the LORD." LOGOS: "every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD." PNEUMA: "Everything devoted under the ban is most holy to the LORD." Ruling: "every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD" — EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "under the ban" which is an explanatory gloss.

**V. 29 — "Who is devoted for destruction."** EMET: "who is devoted [yucharam — Hophal] for destruction / from mankind." LOGOS: "who is to be devoted for destruction from mankind." PNEUMA: "who has been devoted to destruction under the ban." Ruling: "who is devoted for destruction" — drawing on EMET/LOGOS. The phrase min-ha-adam ("from mankind") in the MT specifies that this cherem is from among human beings (as opposed to animals). LOGOS's "from mankind" is preserved. PNEUMA's "under the ban" is an added gloss.

**V. 29 — "Shall be redeemed" vs. "shall be ransomed."** EMET: "shall not be redeemed [lo yifdeh — Niphal of p-d-h]." LOGOS: "shall be ransomed." PNEUMA: "may be ransomed." Ruling: "shall be redeemed" — EMET. The verb is p-d-h (redeem/ransom); both "redeemed" and "ransomed" are defensible. The consensus uses "redeemed" throughout the chapter for ga'al and "buy back" / "redeem" for p-d-h; maintaining "redeemed" here for the negative keeps the chapter's vocabulary consistent.

**V. 29 — "He shall surely be put to death."** EMET: "he shall surely be put to death [mot yumat]." LOGOS: "he shall surely be put to death." PNEUMA: "He must be put to death." Ruling: "he shall surely be put to death" — EMET/LOGOS. The infinitive absolute mot yumat ("dying he shall die") requires the intensifying "surely." PNEUMA's "must be" loses this force.

**V. 30 — "And every tithe of the land."** EMET: "And all the tithe of the land [ve-khol ma'aser ha-aretz]." LOGOS: "Every tithe of the land." PNEUMA: "Every tithe of the land." Ruling: "And every tithe of the land" — EMET. The vav-conjunction opening the verse is present in the MT.

**V. 30 — "From the seed of the land, from the fruit of the tree."** EMET: "from the seed of the land, from the fruit of the tree." LOGOS: "whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees." PNEUMA: "whether from the seed of the ground or the fruit of the trees." Ruling: "from the seed of the land, from the fruit of the tree" — EMET. The MT's mi-zera' ha-aretz mi-peri ha-etz uses "tree" (ha-etz, singular collective), not "trees." LOGOS and PNEUMA use "trees" (plural); EMET's singular is more precise. "Land" (EMET/LOGOS) for aretz is preferred over PNEUMA's "ground."

**V. 30 — "It belongs to the LORD; it is holy to the LORD."** EMET: "it belongs to the LORD [la-YHWH hu] — it is holy to the LORD [qodesh la-YHWH]." LOGOS: "is the LORD's; it is holy to the LORD." PNEUMA: "belongs to the LORD. It is holy to the LORD." Ruling: "it belongs to the LORD; it is holy to the LORD" — EMET/PNEUMA. The MT's la-YHWH hu is "to the LORD it [is]," conveying belonging; "is the LORD's" (LOGOS) is equivalent but slightly less direct.

**V. 31 — "And if a man redeems some of his tithe."** EMET: "And if a man redeems some of his tithe [ve-im ga'ol yig'al ish mi-ma'asero]." LOGOS: "If a man wishes to redeem some of his tithe." PNEUMA: "If anyone wants to buy back any of their tithe." Ruling: "And if a man redeems some of his tithe" — EMET. The infinitive absolute again; "wishes to" (LOGOS) and "wants to" (PNEUMA) add desiderative nuance not in the MT.

**V. 31 — "He shall add a fifth to it."** EMET: "then he shall add a fifth to it [ve-chamishito yosif alav]." LOGOS: "he shall add a fifth to it." PNEUMA: "they must add a fifth to it." Ruling: "he shall add a fifth to it" — EMET/LOGOS. Third-person singular "he" matches the MT's masculine singular subject; PNEUMA's "they" introduces a plural.

**V. 32 — "Of every tithe of the herd and the flock."** EMET: "And of all the tithe of the herd and the flock [ve-khol-ma'aser baqar va-tson]." LOGOS: "And every tithe of herds and flocks." PNEUMA: "As for every tithe of herd and flock." Ruling: "And of every tithe of the herd and the flock" — EMET. "The herd" and "the flock" (with articles) are more precise than LOGOS's "herds and flocks" (without articles and pluralized).

**V. 32 — "Every tenth animal of all that passes under the rod."** EMET: "of all that passes under the rod [tachat ha-shevet]." LOGOS: "every tenth animal of all that pass under the herdsman's staff." PNEUMA: "every tenth animal that passes under the shepherd's rod." Ruling: "every tenth animal of all that passes under the rod" — EMET/adjusted. The MT's ha-shevet is "the rod/staff/scepter" — "rod" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adequate and direct; "herdsman's staff" (LOGOS) adds "herdsman's" which is not in the MT.

**V. 33 — "He shall not inspect whether it is good or bad."** EMET: "He shall not look to see whether it is good or bad [bein tov le-ra]." LOGOS: "One shall not differentiate between good or bad." PNEUMA: "The owner must not inspect the animals to find good ones or bad ones." Ruling: "He shall not inspect whether it is good or bad" — EMET/adjusted. The MT's lo yevaqer (Pi'el of b-q-r, "to examine/inspect/discriminate") is well rendered by "inspect." LOGOS's "differentiate" is accurate but more abstract. PNEUMA adds "the animals" and "to find" which are not in the MT.

**V. 33 — "He shall not substitute for it."** EMET: "and he shall not exchange it [ve-lo yamirennu]." LOGOS: "neither shall he make a substitute for it." PNEUMA: "and must not make any exchange." Ruling: "and he shall not substitute for it" — adjusted. The verb yamir (Hifil of m-w-r) is the same as v. 10's second verb; "substitute" is consistent with v. 10.

**V. 33 — "If he does indeed substitute for it" — intensifying infinitive.** EMET: "and if he does indeed exchange it [ve-im hamor yamirennu]." LOGOS: "and if he does make a substitute for it." PNEUMA: "If he does make an exchange." Ruling: "and if he does indeed substitute for it" — EMET. The infinitive absolute hamor demands the intensifying "does indeed"; LOGOS's "does make" and PNEUMA's "does make" are weaker.

**V. 33 — "Then it and its substitute shall both be holy — it shall not be redeemed."** EMET: "then it and its substitute shall both be holy — it shall not be redeemed [lo yig'al]." LOGOS: "both it and the substitute shall be holy; it shall not be redeemed." PNEUMA: "both the original animal and its substitute become holy and cannot be bought back." Ruling: "then it and its substitute shall both be holy — it shall not be redeemed" — EMET. The dash (reflecting the MT's structural pause before lo yig'al) and "it shall not be redeemed" (EMET/LOGOS) are preferred over PNEUMA's "cannot be bought back."

**V. 34 — "The commandments that the LORD commanded Moses."** EMET: "These are the commandments [ha-mitsvot] that the LORD commanded Moshe for the sons of Yisra'el on Mount Sinai." LOGOS: "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai." PNEUMA: "These are the commandments that the LORD gave to Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai." Ruling: "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai" — LOGOS. The verb tsivvah (Pi'el of ts-w-h) means "commanded/charged/commissioned" — "commanded" (EMET/LOGOS) is the standard rendering; "gave to" (PNEUMA) flattens the commanding authority into a neutral gift-transfer. "Sons of Israel" (EMET/LOGOS) is the project term for bney yisrael; "people of Israel" (PNEUMA) is a less precise rendering. "Moses" — English name form per project convention.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On ki yafli neder (v. 2):** The Hifil yafli from p-l-' ("to be extraordinary") designates this as a vow of special or extraordinary character — not routine vow-making but a particularly solemn dedication. EMET and LOGOS both render "makes a special vow." PNEUMA's "extraordinary vow" is etymologically precise but "special vow" has the stronger formal-equivalence tradition (cf. NASB, NIV, ESV margin). The consensus preserves "special vow."

**On erekh / erkekha throughout (vv. 2–27):** The term erekh (your valuation / assessed value) is addressed to the priest as the assessor. It appears consistently as "valuation" throughout — the technical term for the fixed monetary equivalents of the dedication. "Your valuation" is maintained in all cases where erkekha appears with the second-person suffix (addressed to the priest), consistent with the project's practice of preserving the priestly-legal register.

**On the fifth (chamishito, vv. 13, 15, 19, 27, 31):** The 20% surcharge recurs five times. "A fifth" is the consistent rendering — literal (one-fifth of the value), natural in English, and established across formal-equivalence tradition. The surcharge discourages casual vow-making and commodification of the sacred sphere.

**On cherem (vv. 21, 28, 29):** The project ruling "devoted thing" is maintained. The LXX renders anathema (ἀνάθεμα) throughout — this later became Paul's strongest term of exclusion (Rom 9:3; 1 Cor 16:22; Gal 1:8–9). The Hebrew term is preserved as "devoted thing/devoted" without gloss in the translation text.

**On qodesh-qodashim (v. 28):** "Most holy" — the superlative construction applied here to the cherem — is the same grade of holiness applied to the inner sanctuary, the Ark, and the sin offering. The consensus preserves "most holy" as established throughout Leviticus.

**On the firstborn (v. 26) and the tithe (vv. 30–32):** Both represent a pre-existing divine claim, not a voluntary dedication. The firstborn "belongs to the LORD" already (Exod 13); the tithe "is the LORD's" already (v. 30). Neither can be "dedicated" as a vow because they are not the owner's to give. The chapter structurally distinguishes between what can be vowed (vv. 2–25), what cannot be vowed because it already belongs to YHWH (vv. 26, 30), and what lies beyond all redemption (vv. 28–29).

**On mot yumat (v. 29):** The doubled death-formula ("shall surely be put to death"), familiar from Leviticus 20, recurs here for the devoted person under cherem. The intensifying infinitive absolute mot is preserved through "surely" per the project's established convention.

**On the closing colophon (v. 34):** This is the final verse of the entire book of Leviticus. The formula elleh ha-mitsvot asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh el-benei Yisrael be-har Sinai — "These are the commandments that the LORD commanded Moses for the sons of Israel on Mount Sinai" — is the broader and more comprehensive of the two book-closing colophons (cf. 26:46 which used "statutes and judgments and torot"). Ha-mitsvot ("the commandments") gathers the entirety of Leviticus under a single authoritative claim. The final word of the book — be-har Sinai, "on Mount Sinai" — grounds all Levitical legislation in the covenant moment at the mountain. Leviticus ends where it theologically began.

**On the LXX (Vaticanus, pp. 113–114):** The LXX renders erekh as timê ("valuation/price") throughout; cherem as anathema consistently in vv. 28–29; mot yumat as thanatô apothaneitai ("shall die by death") — preserving the Hebrew cognate-infinitive construction; the colophon at v. 34 as autai hai entolai has eneteilato Kyrios tô Môysê pros tous huious Israêl en tô orei Sinai. No significant textual variants affect the sense of the chapter in the Vaticanus.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 27:1–34 — all 34 verses rendered. Final chapter of Leviticus. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_027.md`.*
